content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 9580.4 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Chr istine Reed 
Councilmember 
City of Santa Monica 
1685 Main Street 
Administration 
322·5660 
Santa Monica, CA 
90401 
Dear Ms. Reed: 
Executive/Legal 
322·5901 
May 23, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-O<U1 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice, Our No. A-83-114 
Your letter requesting our written advice relates the 
following facts and questions for our consideration. 
FACTS 
You have just completed a successful campaign for 
re-election to the City Council. 
You also sit as an appointed 
member of the Los Angeles County Transportation Commission 
(LACTC) and the Regional Water Quality Control Board. 
A controlled campaign committee supporting you and two other 
candidates received a contribution of $1,000 from each of two 
companies which are subsidiaries of DMJM (Daniel Mann Johnson & 
Mendenhall), a large engineering and architectural firm in 
Los Angeles. 
DMJM actively competes for design/build contracts for light 
and heavy rail projects. 
The LACTC issues such contracts. 
QUESTIONS 
You have asked several questions related to the application 
of AB 1040 (Levine), Government Code Section 84308,1/ to these 
circumstances and other situations. 
1/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise specified. 


Christine Reed 
May 23, 1983 
Page 2 
1. 
If DMJM is a bidder for a LACTC contract, are you 
required to abstain from voting under AB 1040? 
Does the term 
"applicant" in AB 1040 include all those who bid to provide 
services to the LACTC? 
2. 
Does this law also apply to those elected officials who 
sit as members of the Board of Regents of the University of 
California or who sit as members of the Metropolitan Water 
District Board of Directors or the Regional Water Quality 
Control Board? 
ANALYSIS 
Generally, AB 1040 (Levine) places certain restrictions upon 
actions by public officials, serving on appointed bodies, with 
respect to those who have given, or who would give, campaign 
contributions of $250 or more. 
The Fair Political Practices 
Commission has adopted regulations interpreting the provisions 
of AB 1040. 
I attach a copy for your convenience. 
Turning now 
to your specific questions, we will take each in turn. 
1. 
Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.2(b) provides 
as follows: 
The awarding of a contract or other action taken 
for the purpose of entering into a contract for the 
provision of goods or services to, or employment by, an 
agency, is not an "application for a license, permit or 
other entitlement for use." 
Because a contract for services is not an "application for a 
license, permit or other entitlement for use" it is not covered 
by Section 84308 and you would not be required to abstain from 
voting on the matter. 
2. 
The provisions of Section 84308 apply to elected 
officials who are appointed to other boards and commissions such 
as the Regents of the University of California and the Board of 
Directors of the Metropolitan Water District of Southern 
California and the Regional Water Quality Control Board. 
(See 
enclosed copy of Advice Letter to Jarlath Oley, No. A-83-074.) 
When the Regional Water Quality Control Board undertakes actions 
involving applications for licenses, permits, or other 
entitlements for use, you will be subject to Section 84308's 
provisions. 


Christine Reed 
May 23, 1983 
Page 3 
Should you have any questions regarding this letter, I may 
be reached at 916/322-5901. 
REL: krn 
Enclosures 
/ 
--7~''" --( 
Robert E. Le1digh 
Counsel, Legal Division 


SIXTEEN EIGHTY FIVE MAIN STREET 
SANTA MONICA, CALI 
RNIA 90401 
April 20, 1983 
Mr. Robert E. Leidigh 
Counsel, Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.o. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Mr. Leidigh: 
Re: 
Your letter 2/28/83 to Ron Schneider 
regarding AB 1040 and L.A.C.T.C. 
I have reviewed your letter to Ron and his report of same to us. 
I have just completed a successful campaign for re-election to the 
City Council and am concerned about AB 1040. 
Neither your letter nor Ronls report is entirely clear to me, so I 
will cite a specific example and ask if it is covered by AB 1040. 
A controlled campaign committee supporting me and two other candi-
dates received a contribution of $1000 from each of two companies 
which are subsidiaries of DMJM (Daniel Mann Mendenhall & Johnson), 
a large engineering and architectural firm in Los Angeles. 
DMJM actively competes for design/build contracts for light and 
heavy rail projects. 
The LACTC issues such contracts. 
If DMJM 
is a bidder for such a contract, am I required to abstain under 
AB 1040? 
Does the term lIapplicantll in the law include all those 
who bid to provide services to the Commission? 
I would appreciate your attention to these questions. 
I have to 
set up some sort of record keeping system to track contributors, 
since I am also a member of the Regional Water Quality Control 
Board. 
I need to know how extensive my control system should be. 
Does this law also apply to those elected officials who sit as 
members of the Board of Regents of the University of California? 
Does it cover the Metropolitan Water District Board Members? 
CR:sc 
cc: 
Rick Richmond, Executive Director LACTC 
Ron Schneider, Deputy County Counsel 


Dear 
answer

You have asked several questions related to the application 
of AB 1040 (Levine), Government Code Section 84308,1/ to these 
circumstances and other situations.

Christine Reed 
May 23, 1983  
1. 
If DMJM is a bidder for a LACTC contract, are you 
required to abstain from voting under AB 1040? 
Does the term 
"applicant" in AB 1040 include all those who bid to provide 
services to the LACTC? 
2. 
Does this law also apply to those elected officials who 
sit as members of the Board of Regents of the University of 
California or who sit as members of the Metropolitan Water 
District Board of Directors or the Regional Water Quality 
Control Board?
sections.conclusion: 1. Because a contract for services is not an "application for a license, permit or other entitlement for use" it is not covered by Section 84308 and you would not be required to abstain from voting on the matter. 2. The provisions of Section 84308 apply to elected officials who are appointed to other boards and commissions such as the Regents of the University of California and the Board of Directors of the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California and the Regional Water Quality Control Board. When the Regional Water Quality Control Board undertakes actions involving applications for licenses, permits, or other entitlements for use, you will be subject to Section 84308's provisions.

Generally, AB 1040 (Levine) places certain restrictions upon 
actions by public officials, serving on appointed bodies, with 
respect to those who have given, or who would give, campaign 
contributions of $250 or more. 
The Fair Political Practices 
Commission has adopted regulations interpreting the provisions

I attach a copy for your convenience. 
Turning now 
to your specific questions, we will take each in turn. 
1.

The awarding of a contract or other action taken

2.

California and the Regional Water Quality Control Board.

Christine Reed 
May 23, 1983
sections.question_synthetic: Does AB 1040 require an elected official to abstain from voting when a contractor who contributed to her campaign bids for contracts before a board on which she serves, and does this law apply to various appointed boards including UC Regents, Metropolitan Water District, and Regional Water Quality Control Board?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: AB 1040 does not apply to contract bidding before the LACTC because contracts are not "applications for licenses, permits, or other entitlements for use." However, AB 1040 does apply to elected officials serving on appointed boards like the UC Regents, Metropolitan Water District, and Regional Water Quality Control Board when those bodies consider applications for licenses, permits, or entitlements.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated April 20, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (dated May 23, 1983). The FPPC response clearly identifies Questions and Analysis/Conclusion sections. High confidence in extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked several questions related to the application 
of AB 1040 (Levine), Government Code Section 84308,1/ to these 
circumstances and other situations. 
1/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise specified. 


Christine Reed 
May 23, 1983  
1. 
If DMJM is a bidder for a LACTC contract, are you 
required to abstain from voting under AB 1040? 
Does the term 
"applicant" in AB 1040 include all those who bid to provide 
services to the LACTC? 
2. 
Does this law also apply to those elected officials who 
sit as members of the Board of Regents of the University of 
California or who sit as members of the Metropolitan Water 
District Board of Directors or the Regional Water Quality 
Control Board?

CONCLUSION: 1. Because a contract for services is not an "application for a license, permit or other entitlement for use" it is not covered by Section 84308 and you would not be required to abstain from voting on the matter. 2. The provisions of Section 84308 apply to elected officials who are appointed to other boards and commissions such as the Regents of the University of California and the Board of Directors of the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California and the Regional Water Quality Control Board. When the Regional Water Quality Control Board undertakes actions involving applications for licenses, permits, or other entitlements for use, you will be subject to Section 84308's provisions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Christine Reed, a Santa Monica Councilmember and appointed member of LACTC and the Regional Water Quality Control Board, that AB 1040 does not require her to abstain from voting on contract awards to companies that contributed to her campaign, but the law does apply to her voting on permit and license applications on other appointed boards.
